sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient presents with right shoulder pain that has been present for a few weeks, initially occurring after a period of heavy physical activity in the backyard, including lifting and moving heavy objects.
The pain is located on the outside of the shoulder and is tender to touch, with some pain when raising the arm.
The patient has had some similar pain in the past, specifically after injuring the same shoulder playing baseball when younger.
The pain is not constant, but rather occurs when the arm is moved.
The patient has not experienced any numbness in the arm or hands.
The patient has been taking Advil and icing the area to alleviate the pain, with some relief.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of previous injury to the right shoulder, specifically a past injury playing baseball.
Review of systems: The patient reports soreness in both feet, particularly in the ankle joints, which he attributes to recent yard work.
Current medications: The patient has been taking Advil for pain relief.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 116/84, pulse rate is 62, respirations are 16, oxygen saturation is 99%, and temperature is 97.8 degrees.
Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, with tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus.
The patient has a good hand grip, and neurovascular exam of the right arm is intact, with capillary refill less than 3 seconds and sensation to touch intact.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder x-ray shows no fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy, which means the patient has injured the tendons of the muscles that help make the shoulder muscles.
Problem list: Rotator cuff tendinopathy; Sore feet; [status: active]; [status: unknown]
Plan:
I referred the patient to physical therapy for approximately six to eight weeks.
I also ordered an MRI to further evaluate the shoulder.
I recommended the patient continue taking Tylenol for pain relief.
If the patient's symptoms do not improve, I suggested considering a steroid injection for the shoulder.
I also advised the patient to try soaking their feet in Epsom salts to alleviate soreness in the feet.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me after physical therapy to assess progress.
